Continued
UPDATES
Updates in Version 2.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Rectal Cancer:
REC-3A
âą Text box modified: For stage II and III, if somatic PI3K pathway alterations, start aspirin (unless contraindicated) 100â162 mg PO daily for 3 years after 
recovery from surgery (concurrent with chemotherapy if given). Therefore, Molecular profiling, to include somatic PI3K pathway alterations, should be 
done on all stage II and III CRC tumors. (Also for REC-5, REC-6, REC-14)

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
Continued
UPDATES
Updates in Version 1.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Rectal Cancer:
REC-1
âą Workup, footnote c added: Refer to the Oncology Guidelines Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health. See Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines for Distress Management (DIS-A).
âą Branches for pedunculated polyp vs sessile polyp removed
Footnote i revised: Observation may be considered, with the understanding that there is significantly greater incidence of adverse outcomes (residual
disease, recurrent disease, mortality, or hematogenous metastasis, but not lymph node metastasis) than pedunculated malignant polyps. Historical 
data have suggested that endoscopically resected sessile malignant polyps with submucosal invasion harbored higher risk of residual disease and/
or lymph node metastases than similar lesions that were of pedunculated morphology. More recently, evidence suggests that margin status and the 
presence or absence of unfavorable histologic features may be the most important predictors of residual disease/ lymph node metastases regardless 
of morphologic type. See Principles of Pathologic Review (REC-B) - Endoscopically Removed Malignant Polyps.
REC-2
âą Workups separated onto separate pages
Workup, Rectal cancer without suspected or proven distant metastases
 âBullet 3 revised: Consider PIK3CA Testing for somatic PI3K pathway alterations for stage II-III
 âFootnote k revised: The rectum lies below a virtual line the peritoneal reflection from the sacral promontory to the upper edge of the symphysis as 
determined by MRI. (Also for REC-3, REC-3A, REC-A 1 of 5)
 âFootnote l modified: FDA added a Black Boxed warning to the capecitabine label, recommending to test patients for genetic variants of DPYD prior
to initiating capecitabine unless immediate treatment is necessary. For information on DPYD testing and fluoropyrimidine-associated toxicity, see 
the Oncology Guidelines for Colon Cancer. (Also for REC-2A)
 âFootnote m added: Somatic PI3K pathway alterations include mutations in PI3KCA exon 9 and 20; other PIK3CA, PI3K3R1, and PTEN mutations; 
and deep deletions of PTEN. Aspirin should not be initiated until after recovery from surgery. For patients receiving adjuvant therapy and aspirin, 
aspirin can be given concurrently with adjuvant chemotherapy. (Also for REC-3A, REC-5, REC-6, REC-14) 
REC-2A
âą Workup, Rectal cancer with suspected or proven distant metastases
Bullet 6 and sub-bullets revised: 
 âMolecular testing Biomarker testing ordered as rapidly as possible following diagnosis, including:
â RAS and BRAF V600E mutations; HER2 (ERBB2) overexpression/amplifications; MMR or MSI status (if not previously done)
â Testing should be conducted as part of multigene panel testing (MGPT) broad molecular profiling, which would identify rare and actionable 
mutations and fusions such as POLE/POLD1, RET, and NTRK 1/2/3.
 âBiomarker terminology changes have been applied throughout 
âą Footnote n revised: Tissue- or blood-based analysis using MGPT next-generation sequencing (NGS) panels have the ability to pick up rare and 
actionable mutations and fusions.
REC-3
âą Clinical stage, branches revised: 
T1 (favorable risk), N0
 âBranches for nonsurgical candidate vs surgical candidate removed
 âPrimary treatment revised: Endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD) or Transanal local excision, if appropriate or Transabdominal resection

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
Continued
UPDATES
Updates in Version 1.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Rectal Cancer:
REC-3 continued
âą T1 (high risk),T2, N0
Primary treatment option modified: Transabdominal resection (preferred)
âą Footnote removed: T1â2, N0 should be based on assessment of pelvis MRI (preferred) or endorectal ultrasound.
âą Footnote removed: Bolus fluorouracil/leucovorin/RT is an option for patients not able to tolerate capecitabine or infusional fluorouracil. (Also for REC-4, 
REC-5, REC-6, REC-8, REC-9, REC-11A, REC-14, REC-16, REC-D 1 of 2)
âą Footnote r modified: ...the 5-year follow-up of the RAPIDO trial now indicates a statistically higher locoregional recurrence rate (10%) in the 
experimental arm of with short-course RT. â chemotherapy â surgery versus control arm (6%) of chemoRT â surgery â adjuvant chemotherapy. 
(Also for REC-4, REC-6)
âą Footnote s modified: In those patients who achieve a complete clinical response with no evidence of residual disease on DRE, rectal MRI, and direct 
endoscopic evaluation, a âwatch and wait,â nonoperative (chemotherapy and/or RT) management approach may be considered in... (Also for REC-4, 
REC-6, REC-14)
REC-3A
âą Text box added: For stage II and III, if somatic PI3K pathway alterations, start aspirin (unless contraindicated) 100â162 mg PO daily for 3 years after 
recovery from surgery (concurrent with chemotherapy if given). Therefore, molecular profiling should be done on all stage II and III CRC tumors. (Also 
for REC-5, REC-6, REC-14)
âą Footnote removed: For stage II-III disease, if PIK3CA mutation, add aspirin 100-162 mg PO daily for 3 years following surgery. (Also for REC-5, REC-6, 
REC-14)
REC-5 
âą pT3, N0, M0, adjuvant treatment modified: Consider FOLFOX or CAPEOX alone or Observation
Footnote w added: The use of postoperative RT should be highly selective (eg, for incomplete mesorectal grade) due to severe toxicity concerns.
REC-8
âą Footnote cc revised: Resection is preferred over locally ablative procedures for liver metastases (eg, image-guided thermal ablation or stereotactic body 
RT [SBRT]). For small liver lesions (â€3 cm), thermal ablation is equivalent to resection. However, these Other local techniques can be considered for 
liver or lung oligometastases when thermal ablation is not indicated (REC-C and REC-E). For small lesions (â€3 cm), thermal ablation is equivalent to 
resection. (Also for REC-9, REC-12, REC-13, REC-16, REC-17)
âą Footnote dd added: For lung metastases, surgery, thermal ablation, and stereotactic RT (SBRT) can be considered (REC-C and REC-E). (Also for 
REC-9, REC-12, REC-13, REC-16, REC-17)
REC-9
âą Footnote removed and added to REC-F 1 of 17: An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for any recommended systemic biologic 
therapy in the Oncology Guidelines. (Also for REC-13) 
âą Footnote ee modified: ...The use of bevacizumab may interfere with wound healing. Similarly, the combination of bevacizumab and SBRT has been 
associated with an increased risk of pulmonary hemorrhage, particularly for ultracentral tumors.
REC-11
âą Footnote mm modified: Determination of tumor gene status for RAS and BRAF mutations and HER2 (ERBB2) overexpression/amplifications 
(individually or as part of tissue- or blood-based MGPT next-generation sequencing [NGS] panel). See Principles of Pathologic Review (REC-B)- KRAS, 
NRAS, and BRAF Mutation Testing and MSI or MMR Testing. NGS panels MGPT has the ability to pick up rare and actionable mutations and fusions. 
(Also for REC-11A)

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
Continued
UPDATES
Updates in Version 1.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Rectal Cancer:
REC-13
âą pMMR/MSS Unresectable Metachronous Metastases
Initial Treatment, regimen modified to allow for additional options for chemotherapy: Encorafenib + (Cetuximab or Panitumumab) Â± FOLFIRI or 
FOLFOX or CAPEOX (BRAF V600E mutation)
 âFootnote ss added: The addition of FOLFOX or CAPEOX can be considered for patients without residual neuropathy and good prior tolerance of 
chemotherapy.
Lapatinib removed as a treatment option (Also for REC-F) 
REC-14
âą dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1; Neoadjuvant/Definitive Immunotherapy
Checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy options modified to include all checkpoint inhibitors on REC-F 5 of 17 as options
Additional branch added after Re-evaluate disease status every 2â3 mo: If consistent response, continue checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy
Footnote removed: If no previous treatment with a checkpoint inhibitor. (Also for REC-16)
REC-16
âą dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1; Neoadjuvant Treatment
Branch modified: Consider holding radiation if complete response to neoadjuvant therapy or If no complete response, consider:...
Footnote removed: Checkpoint inhibitor therapy options include: nivolumab Â± ipilimumab, pembrolizumab, dostarlimab-gxly, cemiplimab-rwlc, 
retifanlimab-dlwr, toripalimabtpzi, or tislelizumab-jsgr. Combination PD-L1/CTLA-4 blockade has been associated with improved survival in 
unresectable or metastatic CRC, but also higher toxicity. (Also for REC-17)
REC-17
âą dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1; Resectable Metachronous Metastases
'Preferred' removed for FOLFOX and CAPEOX
Capecitabine or Fluorouracil/Leucovorin removed as treatment options 
Initial Treatment, No previous immunotherapy modified: Checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy (preferred) (REC-F 5 of 17)
REC-18
âą New page added to algorithm for dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 unresectable metastases with content from previous page in REC-F
REC-A 1 of 5
âą Principles of Imaging; Initial Workup/Staging
Bullet 1 modified: Pelvis MRI with or without contrast or endorectal ultrasound (only for T1 tumors or if MRI is contraindicated)...
Bullet removed: Pelvis MRI or CT can be used for workup of synchronous metastatic disease.
Bullet 2, sub-bullet removed: Evaluate local extent of tumor or infiltration into surrounding structures.
Bullet 2, sub-bullet 4 modified: ...IV iodinated contrast material is may also be contraindicated, and...
REC-A 2 of 5
âą Restaging
Bullet 2 modified: FDG-PET/CT is not usually indicated; however, it may be necessary in case of equivocal additional imaging findings.
âą Follow-up/Surveillance
Bullet 2, sub-bullet 3 modified: FDG-PET/CT examination is usually not recommended.

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
Continued
UPDATES
Updates in Version 1.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Rectal Cancer:
REC-A 2 of 5 continued
âą Follow-up/Surveillance
Bullet 3, sub-bullet 3 added: If liver-directed therapy is contemplated, magnetic resonance with hepatobiliary agent should be considered to better 
delineate number and location of lesions.
Bullet 4 modified: FDG-PET/CT is usually not indicated with the exception of...
Bullet 5 and sub-bullets added: Therapy-dependent follow-up interval recommendations
 âFor chemo/RT: Initial assessment 12 weeks after start of treatment, reassessment 16â20 weeks after start of treatment.
 âFor total neoadjuvant therapy: Response assessment at 24 weeks after start of treatment, reassessment 34â38 weeks after the start of treatment.
REC-A 3 of 5
âą Pelvic MRI Requirements: Criteria updated throughout the tables. (Also for Pelvic MRI Reporting before/after neoadjuvant therapy on REC-A 4 of 5)
REC-A 5 of 5
âą References have been updated.
REC-B 1 of 10 through REC-B 10 of 10
âą Principles of Pathologic and Molecular Review
This section has been extensively revised. Terminology and references have been updated throughout.
REC-C 4 of 9
âą Principles of Surgery and Locoregional Therapies; Criteria for Resectability of Metastases and Locoregional Therapies Within Surgery
Text added: Local therapies for liver and lung oligometastases should be selected through multidisciplinary review based on technical feasibility, 
patient comorbidities, and disease distribution.
Liver, bullet 13 added: For liver metastases, in general, resection may be prioritized over local thermal ablation. However, for select patients with small 
lesions (â€3 cm), thermal ablation should be prioritized, when feasible, due to equivalent outcomes and reduced toxicity compared to surgery.34 SBRT 
can be considered in select cases where other local therapy options are not possible or in patients who prefer a noninvasive approach.
Lung, bullet 10 added: For lung metastases, surgery, thermal ablation, and SBRT can be considered based on multidisciplinary discussion and patient 
preference.
REC-C 8 of 9
âą Reference added: van der Lei S, Puijk RS, Dijkstra M, et al. Thermal ablation versus surgical resection of small-size colorectal liver metastases 
(COLLISION): an international, randomised, controlled, phase 3 non-inferiority trial. Lancet Oncol 2025;26:187-199.
REC-E 1 of 3
âą Principles of Radiation Therapy
General Principles
 âBullet 2 added: Endorectal brachytherapy can be considered as a means of dose escalation to achieve organ preservation in select patients at 
institutions with experience with this treatment technique.
Treatment Information
 âBullet 3 modified: ...consider use of hyperfractionated pelvic re-irradiation or proton beam therapy if retreatment is planned.

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
UPDATES
Updates in Version 1.2026 of the Oncology Guidelines for Rectal Cancer:
REC-E 2 of 3
âą RT Dosing
Sub-bullet 1 modified: 45â54 Gy in 25â30 fractions to the pelvis. Alternatively, a radiation plan using a simultaneous integrated boost can be used to 
deliver 45 Gy to the pelvis and 50 Gy to the tumor bed in 25 fractions or 45 Gy to the pelvis and 54 Gy to the tumor bed in 27 fractions.
Short-course RT, diamond added: Small bowel max point dose should be limited to D0.03 cc 26.5 Gy, and V20 Gy should be 50 cc for individual small 
bowel loops. Depending upon anatomy and clinical circumstances, it may be reasonable to accept a D0.03cc up to 28.75 Gy.
REC-E 3 of 3
âą References have been updated.
REC-F
âą Principles of Systemic Therapy for Advanced or Metastatic Disease
This section has been extensively revised. Treatment recommendations have been broken down by biomarker status and put into new tables.
 âLanding page added
 âBullet list of principles added on REC-F 1 of 17 
 âDosing and references updated
REC-F 4 of 17
âą KRAS WT/NRAS WT/BRAF V600E Mutation
Initial therapy, Preferred
 âRegimen added as a category 2A recommendation: FOLFIRI/Encorafenib + (Cetuximab or Panitumumab)
Initial therapy, Other Recommended
 âRegimen added as a category 2A recommendation: CAPEOX/Encorafenib + (Cetuximab or Panitumumab)
REC-F 5 of 17
âą Checkpoint Inhibitor Therapy for Any Line of Therapy
The following agents have been added as category 2A recommendations:
 âPD-1
â Penpulimab-kcqx
 âPD-L1
â Atezolizumab
â Avelumab
â Cosibelimab-ipdl
â Durvalumab
Asterisk modified: Patients should be followed closely for 10 weeks to assess for response. Some of these agents have not been specifically studied 
in CRC but are included in this Guideline based on the presumed class effect from data demonstrated in other cancer types. See Discussion section 
for more information.
REC-G 1 of 2
âą Principles of Survivorship; Counseling Regarding Healthy Lifestyle and Wellness
Bullet 3 modified: Adopt a physically active lifestyle (at least 30 minutes of moderateintensity activity on most days of the week). Discuss and facilitate 
options to achieve a goal of at least 150 minutes of moderate to vigorous physical activity per week.
Bullet 7: Link added to Oncology Guidelines for Smoking Cessation

Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
Pedunculated 
or sessile polyp 
(adenoma) with 
invasive cancer
REC-1
a	All patients with rectal cancer should be counseled for family history. Patients 
with suspected Lynch syndrome (LS), familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP), 
and attenuated FAP, see the Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk 
Assessment: Colorectal, Endometrial, and Gastric.
b	For melanoma histology, see the Oncology Guidelines for Melanoma: Cutaneous.
c Refer to the Oncology Guidelines Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes 
social determinants of health. See Oncology Guidelines for Distress Management 
(DIS-A).
d Confirm the presence of invasive cancer (pT1). pTis has no biological potential to 
metastasize.
e It has not been established if molecular markers (other than MSI-high [MSI-H]/
MMR deficient [dMMR]) are useful in treatment determination (predictive markers) 
and prognosis. Compton CC, et al. Arch Pathol Lab Med 2000;124:979-994.
f Principles of Pathologic Review (REC-B).
g Principles of Surgery and Locoregional Therapies (REC-C).
h Principles of Imaging (REC-A).
i Historical data have suggested that endoscopically resected sessile malignant 
polyps with submucosal invasion harbored higher risk of residual disease and/
or lymph node metastases than similar lesions that were of pedunculated 
morphology. More recently, evidence suggests that margin status and the 
presence or absence of unfavorable histologic features may be the most 
important predictors of residual disease/lymph node metastases regardless of 
morphologic type. See Principles of Pathologic Review (REC-B) - Endoscopically 
Removed Malignant Polyps.

REC-3
a	All patients with rectal cancer should be counseled for family history. Patients with 
suspected LS, FAP, and attenuated FAP, see the Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/Familial 
High-Risk Assessment: Colorectal, Endometrial, and Gastric.
b	For melanoma histology, see the Oncology Guidelines for Melanoma: Cutaneous.
g Principles of Surgery and Locoregional Therapies (REC-C).
h Principles of Imaging (REC-A).
j For tools to aid optimal assessment and care of older adults with cancer, see the Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology.
k The rectum lies below the peritoneal reflection from the sacral promontory to the upper 
edge of the symphysis as determined by MRI. 
o High-risk features include positive margins, lymphovascular invasion, poorly differentiated 
tumors, or sm3 invasion (submucosal invasion to the lower third of the submucosal level).
p Principles of Radiation Therapy (REC-E).
q Evaluation for short-course RT should be in a multidisciplinary setting, with a discussion of 
the need for downstaging and the possibility of long-term toxicity.
r While short-course RT can be considered for preoperative radiation, for high-risk rectal 
cancer (clinical tumor stage cT4a or cT4b, extramural vascular invasion [EMVI], clinical 
nodal stage c-N2, involved mesorectal fascia [MRF], or enlarged lateral lymph nodes 
considered to be metastatic), the 5-year follow-up of the RAPIDO trial now indicates a 
statistically higher locoregional recurrence rate with short-course RT. 
s In those patients who achieve a complete clinical response, a âwatch and wait,â 
nonoperative management approach may be considered in centers with experienced 
multidisciplinary teams. The degree to which risk of local and/or distant recurrence 
may be increased relative to standard surgical resection has not yet been adequately 
characterized. Decisions for nonoperative management (NOM) should involve a careful 
discussion with the patient of their risk tolerance. See Principles of Nonoperative 
Management (REC-H).

ee There should be at least a 6-week interval between the last dose of 
bevacizumab and elective surgery, and re-initiation of bevacizumab should 
be delayed at least 6 to 8 weeks postoperatively. There is an increased risk of 
stroke and other arterial events, especially in those aged â„65 years. The use 
of bevacizumab may interfere with wound healing. Similarly, the combination of 
bevacizumab and SBRT has been associated with an increased risk of pulmonary 
hemorrhage, particularly for ultracentral tumors.

ff Patients with BRAF mutations other than V600E may be considered for anti-EGFR therapy.
pp Hepatic artery infusion Â± systemic chemotherapy (VEGF inhibitor contraindicated) (category 
2B) is also an option at institutions with experience in both the surgical and medical oncologic 
aspects of this procedure.
qq For infection risk, monitoring, and prophylaxis recommendations for targeted therapies, see 
INF-A in the Oncology Guidelines for Prevention and Treatment of Cancer-Related Infections.
rr Bevacizumab is the preferred anti-angiogenic agent based on toxicity and/or cost. 
ss The addition of FOLFOX or CAPEOX can be considered for patients without residual 
neuropathy and good prior tolerance of chemotherapy.
tt Some activity was seen after previous treatment with a HER2-targeted regimen. May not be 
indicated in patients with underlying lung issues due to lung toxicity (drug-related deaths from 
interstitial lung disease were reported in 3.5% of patients on the DESTINY-CRC01 trial).
uu If patient is unable to tolerate EGFR inhibitor due to toxicity, single-agent adagrasib or 
sotorasib can be considered.
vv Biologic therapy is only appropriate for continuation of favorable response from conversion 
therapy.

g Principles of Surgery and Locoregional Therapies (REC-C).
h Principles of Imaging (REC-A).
m Somatic PI3K pathway alterations include mutations in PIK3CA exon 9 and 20; other PIK3CA, PIK3R1, and PTEN mutations; and deep deletions of PTEN. Aspirin should not be initiated 
until after recovery from surgery. For patients receiving adjuvant therapy and aspirin, aspirin can be given concurrently with adjuvant chemotherapy.
p Principles of Radiation Therapy (REC-E).
q Evaluation for short-course RT should be in a multidisciplinary setting, with a discussion of the need for downstaging and the possibility of long-term toxicity.
s In those patients who achieve a complete clinical response, a âwatch and wait,â nonoperative management approach may be considered in centers with experienced multidisciplinary 
teams. The degree to which risk of local and/or distant recurrence may be increased relative to standard surgical resection has not yet been adequately characterized. Decisions for NOM 
should involve a careful discussion with the patient of their risk tolerance. See Principles of Nonoperative Management (REC-H).
t Principles of Perioperative Therapy (REC-D).
v For select patients who may be candidates for IORT, see Principles of Radiation Therapy (REC-E).
z FOLFIRINOX is not recommended in this setting. 
ww In select cases (eg, a patient who is not a candidate for intensive therapy) neoadjuvant therapy with chemo/RT or RT alone may be considered prior to surgery.

header: PRINCIPLES OF IMAGING1
content:
REC-A
1 OF 5
Initial Workup/Staging
âą Pelvis MRI with or without contrast or endorectal ultrasound (only for T1 tumors or if MRI is contraindicated). See Pelvis MRI Requirements (REC-A 3 of 
5) and Reporting (REC-A 4 of 5). 
Assess T and N stage of the primary rectal tumor.
Pelvis MRI can be performed with or without IV gadolinium contrast per institutional preferences.
Pelvis MRI may not be required for local staging if tumor is known to be definite T1 or if patient is not a candidate for primary tumor resection (eg, 
widespread metastases, plan for permanent colonic diversion).
The rectum lies below the peritoneal reflection from the sacral promontory to the upper edge of the symphysis as determined by MRI.
âą Chest CT and abdominal CT or MRI
Assess for distant metastatic disease to lungs, thoracic and abdominal lymph nodes, liver, peritoneal cavity, and other organs.
CT performed with intravenous (IV) iodinated contrast and oral contrast material unless contraindicated. 
IV contrast is not required for the chest CT (but usually given if performed with abdominal CT).
If IV iodinated contrast material is contraindicated because of significant contrast allergy, then MRI examination of the abdomen with IV gadolinium-
based contrast agent (GBCA) can be obtained instead. In patients with chronic renal failure (glomerular filtration rate [GFR] <30 mL/min) who are 
not on dialysis, IV iodinated contrast material may also be contraindicated, and IV GBCA can be administered in select cases using gadofosveset 
trisodium, gadoxetate disodium, gadobenate dimeglumine, or gadoteridol. 
If iodinated and gadolinium contrast are both contraindicated due to significant allergy or chronic renal failure without dialysis, then consider MRI 
without IV contrast or consider FDG-PET/CT imaging.
âą FDG-PET/CT is not routinely indicated.
FDG-PET/CT does not supplant a contrast-enhanced diagnostic CT or MRI and should only be used to evaluate an equivocal finding on a contrast-
enhanced CT or MRI or in patients with strong contraindications to IV contrast administration. 
âą Consider FDG-PET/CT (skull base to mid-thigh):
If potentially surgically curable M1 disease in selected cases.
In patients considered for image-guided liver-directed therapies for liver metastases (ie, thermal ablation, radioembolization).2,3
âą If liver-directed therapy or surgery is contemplated, a hepatic MRI with IV routine extracellular or hepatobiliary GBCA is preferred over CT to assess 
exact number and distribution of metastatic foci for local treatment planning.
References

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
REC-A
2 OF 5
Restaging
âą Chest CT and abdominal CT or MRI and pelvis MRI
Prior to surgery for restaging
Prior to adjuvant treatment to assess response to primary therapy or resection
During re-evaluation of conversion to resectable disease
âą FDG-PET/CT is not usually indicated; however, it may be necessary in case of equivocal additional imaging findings.
Follow-up/Surveillance
âą Stage I disease: 
Imaging is not routinely indicated and should only be based on symptoms and clinical concern for recurrent/metastatic disease.
âą Stage II & III disease: 
C/A/P CT every 6 to 12 months (category 2B for frequency <12 months) for a total of 5 years.
MRI or EUS of the rectum every 3 to 6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for a total of 5 years (for patients with transanal local excision only).
FDG-PET/CT examination is usually not recommended.
âą Stage IV disease: 
C/A/P CT every 3 to 6 months (category 2B for frequency <6 months) x 2 years, then every 6 to 12 months for a total of 5 years.
MRI or EUS of the rectum every 3 to 6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for a total of 5 years (for patients with transanal excision only).
If liver-directed therapy is contemplated, magnetic resonance with hepatobiliary agent should be considered to better delineate number and location of 
lesions.
âą FDG-PET/CT is usually not indicated with the exception of selected patients who are considered for image-guided liver-directed therapies for hepatic 
metastases (ie, thermal ablation, radioembolization) or serial CEA elevation during follow-up.
âą Therapy-dependent follow-up interval recommendations4-10:
For chemo/RT: Initial assessment 12 weeks after start of treatment, reassessment 16â20 weeks after start of treatment.
For total neoadjuvant therapy: Response assessment at 24 weeks after start of treatment, reassessment 34â38 weeks after the start of treatment.

header:
PRINCIPLES OF IMAGING
Pelvic MRI Requirements1
content:
REC-A
3 OF 5
Patient Preparation
Rectal distension with gel
No consensus on the use of spasmolytic agents, endorectal gel, or cleansing enema. Institutional preference 
and protocols have been accepted in regard to these preparations.
Use of spasmolytic agents
No consensus on the use of spasmolytic agents, endorectal gel, or cleansing enema. Institutional preference 
and protocols have been accepted in regard to these preparations.
Use of enema or microenema
No consensus on the use of spasmolytic agents, endorectal gel, or cleansing enema. Institutional preference 
and protocols have been accepted in regard to these preparations.
MRI Hardware Requirement
Magnet strength 
Minimum requirement 1.5 T
Coil
Coil: External phased array coil (endorectal coils expressly NOT recommended)
MRI Sequences
2D high-resolution T2-weighted 
âą Slice thickness 1â3 mm. 3D T2-weighted sequences are not adequate substitutes
âą Main sequences for T staging and detection of pathologic lymph nodes
âą Axial, sagittal, and coronal plane to assess extent and relationship to all surrounding structures
âą Axial and coronal slices should be angulated along the short (perpendicular) and long (parallel) axis of tumor 
for tumors in the middle and upper part of the rectum and along the anal canal for low rectal tumors
T1-weighted without contrast 
T1-weighted images may be helpful for mucinous tumors on staging and restaging, as the signal can be 
similar to fat on T2.
Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI)
The DWI sequence is recommended for both staging and restaging including at least a high b-value of â„800â
1000 (growing evidence of benefit).
T1-weighted with contrast
No consensus for contrast/dynamic-enhanced post-contrast sequences. No conclusive evidence for staging or 
restaging value at the time of consensus, not routinely recommended.
References

header:
PRINCIPLES OF IMAGING
Pelvic MRI Reporting1
content:
REC-A
4 OF 5
At presentation 
(before 
neoadjuvant 
therapy)
âą Distance from the anal verge or anorectal junction to the lower aspect of the tumor 
âą Relationship of tumor to the peritoneal reflection (above/straddles/below)
âą Tumor relationship to the sphincter in low rectal tumors (none/internal/internal and external)
âą Tumor length
âą T-stage of primary mass
âą Tumor deposits within the mesorectum
âą Involvement of the mesorectal fascia (MRF) and the smallest distance (mm) between the tumor and the MRF and its locationa
âą N-stage
âą Presence/absence of suspicious extramesorectal lymph nodes
âą Number of suspicious lymph nodes (N staging)
âą Specify locoregional [internal iliac, obturator, mesorectal (N staging)] versus distant [common and external iliac, retroperitoneal 
(metastatic)] chains.
âą Inguinal nodes may be locoregional if tumor is below the dentate line, or metastatic if tumor is above the dentate line
âą Presence/absence of extramural vascular invasion (EMVI)
âą Additional findings that can be provided in synoptic report:
The circumferential location of the tumor 
Morphologic pattern of tumor growth (eg, annular, polypoid, mucinous, ulcerated, perforated)
After neoadjuvant 
therapy
âą Distance from the anal verge or anorectal junction to the lower aspect of the remaining tumor 
âą Tumor length
âą Presence/absence of a residual tumor (intermediate signal on T2-weighted images)
âą Presence/absence of fibrosis (low signal on T2-weighted images)
âą yT-stage and any remaining tumor deposits within the mesorectum
âą yN-stage and number of remaining suspicious lymph nodes
âą Presence of any remaining suspicious extramesorectal lymph nodes
âą Persistent involvement/regression from the MRFa
âą The smallest distance (mm) between the remaining tumor and the MRF and its location
âą EMVI should be described (if regressed/present)
âą Additional findings that can be provided in synoptic report:
The circumferential location of the remaining tumor within the wall 
The morphologic pattern of tumor growth
a	Circumferential resection margin (CRM) measured at the closest distance of the tumor to the MRF. Clear CRM: Greater than 1 mm from MRF and levator muscles and 
not invading into the intersphincteric plane. Involved CRM: within 1 mm of MRF; or, for lower third rectal tumors, within 1 mm from levator muscle; or, for anal canal 
lesions, invasion into or beyond the intersphincteric plane.
References

REFERENCES
content:
REC-A
5 OF 5
1 ACR manual on contrast media v10.3 https://www.acr.org/-/media/ACR/Files/Clinical-Resources/Contrast_Media.pdf. Accessed May 25, 2017.
2 Mauri G, Gennaro N, De Beni S, et al. Real-time US-18FDG-PET/CT image fusion for guidance of thermal ablation of 18FDG-PET-positive liver metastases: the added 
value of contrast enhancement. Cardiovasc Intervent Radiol 2019;42:60-68.
3 Cornelis FH, Petre EN, Vakiani E, et al. Immediate postablation 18 F-FDG injection and corresponding SUV are surrogate biomarkers of local tumor progression after 
thermal ablation of colorectal carcinoma liver metastases. J Nucl Med 2018;59:1360-1365.
4 Expert Panel on Gastrointestinal Imaging; Korngold EK, Kambadakone AR, Berlin J, et al. ACR Appropriateness CriteriaÂź Staging and Disease Monitoring of Rectal 
Cancer. J Am Coll Radiol 2025;22:S638-S657.
5 Lee S, Kassam Z, Baheti AD, et al. Rectal cancer lexicon 2023 revised and updated consensus statement from the Society of Abdominal Radiology Colorectal and 
Anal Cancer Disease-Focused Panel. Abdom Radiol (NY) 2023;48:2792-2806. 
6 Beets-Tan RGH, Lambregts DMJ, Maas M, et al. Magnetic resonance imaging for clinical management of rectal cancer: Updated recommendations from the 2016 
European Society of Gastrointestinal and Abdominal Radiology (ESGAR) consensus meeting. Eur Radiol 2018;28:1465-1475.
7 Attenberger UI, Clasen S, Ghadimi M, et al. Importance and qualitative requirements of magnetic resonance imaging for therapy planning in rectal cancer - 
Interdisciplinary recommendations of AIO, ARO, ACO and the German Radiological Society. Rofo 2021;193:513-520. 
8 Bregni G, Adams R, Bale R, et al. EORTC consensus recommendations on the optimal management of colorectal cancer liver metastases. Cancer Treat Rev 
2025;136:102926. 
9 Sheedy SP, Arya S, Baheti AD, et al. SAR disease-focused panel: Restaging rectal cancer MRI synoptic report-User guide with imaging review. Radiographics  
2025;45:e250006.
10 Custers PA, Beets GL, Bach SP, et al. An international expert-based consensus on the definition of a clinical near-complete response after neoadjuvant (chemo)
radiotherapy for rectal cancer. Dis Colon Rectum 2024;67:782-795.

Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
Endoscopically Removed Malignant Polyps 
âą A malignant polyp is defined as one with cancer invading through the muscularis mucosae and into the submucosa (pT1). pTis is not considered to be a 
âmalignant polyp.â
âą Favorable histopathologic features: no angiolymphatic invasion, depth of submucosal invasion <1 mm, low tumor budding,1 and negative margin of 
resection. A positive margin has been defined as: tumor cells present at or <1 mm from the transected margin.2-5
âą Unfavorable histologic features, angiolymphatic invasion, depth of submucosal invasion >1 mm, high tumor budding, or a âpositive margin.â See above 
for definition of a positive margin. In several studies, tumor budding has been associated with adverse outcome and may preclude polypectomy as an 
adequate treatment of malignant polyps.
âą There is controversy as to whether malignant colorectal polyps with a sessile configuration can be successfully treated by endoscopic removal. The 
literature seems to indicate that endoscopically removed sessile malignant polyps have a significantly greater incidence of adverse outcome (residual 
disease, recurrent disease, mortality, or hematogenous metastasis, but not lymph node metastasis) than do polypoid malignant polyps. However, when 
one looks closely at the data, configuration by itself is not a significant variable for adverse outcome, and endoscopically removed malignant sessile 
polyps with low-grade histology, <1 mm submucosal invasion, negative margin, and no lymphovascular invasion can be successfully treated with 
endoscopic polypectomy.4-8
Transanal Local Excision 
âą Favorable histopathologic features: <3 cm size, pT1, grade 1 or 2, no lymphatic or venous invasion, or negative margins.9,10
âą Unfavorable histopathologic features: >3 cm in size, >pT1, with grade 3, or lymphovascular invasion, positive margin, high tumor budding, or sm3 
(lower one third of the submucosa) depth of tumor invasion.9-11
Rectal Cancer Appropriate for Resection
âą Histologic confirmation of primary malignant rectal neoplasm.
âą The rectum lies below a virtual line from the sacral promontory to the upper edge of the symphysis as determined by MRI.
REC-B
1 OF 10
References

header: PRINCIPLES OF PATHOLOGIC AND MOLECULAR REVIEW
content:
PLEASE NOTE that use of this Oncology Guidelines Content is governed by the End-User License Agreement, and you MAY NOT distribute this Content or use it with any artificial intelligence model or tool.
Printed by MĂĄximo LĂłpez Chenlo on 5/4/2026 6:29:39 PM. Copyright Â© 2026 Oncology Guidelines, Inc. All Rights Reserved.

Rectal Cancer
Version 2.2026, 04/07/26 Â© 2026 Oncology GuidelinesÂź (Oncology GuidelinesÂź), All rights reserved. Oncology GuidelinesÂź and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
Pathologic Stage
âą The following parameters should be reported:
Grade of the cancer
Depth of invasion (pT), the pT stage, is based on viable tumor. Acellular mucin pools are not considered to be residual tumor in those patients treated 
with neoadjuvant therapy.
Number of lymph nodes evaluated and number positive (N). Acellular mucin pools are not considered to be residual tumor in those patients treated 
with neoadjuvant therapy.
Status of proximal, distal, circumferential (radial), and mesenteric margins12,13
Circumferential resection margin (CRM)14-18
Neoadjuvant treatment effect16,17,19-21
Lymphovascular invasion16,17,22
Perineural invasion (PNI)23-25
Tumor deposits26,27
âą CRM - A positive CRM is defined as tumor â€1 mm from the margin. This assessment includes both tumor within a lymph node as well as direct tumor 
extension. However, if CRM positivity is based solely on intranodal tumor, it should be stated in the pathology report. A positive CRM is a more powerful 
predictor of local recurrence in patients treated with neoadjuvant therapy. A positive CRM secondary to lymph node metastasis in some studies has 
been associated with lower recurrence rates than by direct extension.14-18
âą Neoadjuvant treatment effect - The most recent College of American Pathologists (CAP) Guidelines on examination specimens of the rectum and the 
AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, Eighth Edition require commenting on treatment effect after neoadjuvant therapy. The minimum requirement is:
Treatment effect present.
No definitive response identified.
âą The system used to grade tumor response as recommended by the AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, Eighth Edition and the CAP Guidelines is that as 
modified from Ryan R, et al. Histopathology 2005;47:141-146 and Gavioli M, et al. Dis Colon Rectum 2005;48:1851-1857.
0 - Complete response: No remaining viable cancer cells.
1 - Moderate response: Only small clusters or single cancer cells remaining.
2 - Minimal response: Residual cancer remaining, but with predominant fibrosis.
3 - Poor response: Minimal or no tumor kill; extensive residual cancer. 
According to the CAP, it is optional to grade the tumor response to treatment. However, the Oncology Guidelines Rectal Cancer Guidelines Panel recommends 
grading tumor response. Other grading systems that are used are referenced.16,17,19-21
REC-B
2 OF 10

Discussion
Pathologic Stage (continued)
âą PNI - The presence of PNI is associated with a significantly worse prognosis. In multivariate analysis, PNI has been shown to be an independent 
prognostic factor for cancer-specific, overall, and disease-free survival. For stage II rectal cancer, those with PNI have a significantly worse 5-year 
disease-free survival compared to those without PNI (29% vs. 82%; P = .0005). In stage III rectal cancer, those with PNI have a significantly worse 
prognosis.22-27
âą Tumor deposits - Irregular discrete tumor deposits in pericolic or perirectal fat away from the leading edge of the tumor and showing no evidence of 
residual lymph node tissue, but within the lymphatic drainage of the primary carcinoma, are considered to be tumor deposits or satellite nodules and are 
not counted as lymph nodes replaced by tumor. Most examples are due to lymphovascular invasion or, more rarely, PNI. Because these tumor deposits 
are associated with reduced disease-free and overall survival, their number should be recorded in the surgical pathology report.
âą Tumor budding - In recent years, tumor budding has been identified as a new prognostic factor in colon cancer. Recently, there was an international 
consensus conference on tumor budding reporting.28 A tumor bud is defined as a single cell or a cluster of â€4 cells identified on hematoxylin and 
eosin (H&E) at the advancing edge of the invasive carcinoma. The total number of buds are reported from a selected hot spot measuring 0.785 mm 
(20x ocular in most microscopes/via a conversion factor). Budding is separated into three tiers: low tier (0â4 buds), intermediate tier (5â9 buds), and 
high tier (â„10 buds). Two recent studies29,30 using this scoring system have shown tumor budding to be an independent prognostic factor for stage II 
colon cancer. An American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) guideline for stage II colon cancer designates tumor budding as an adverse (high-risk) 
factor.31 Several studies have shown that high-tier tumor budding in pT1 colorectal cancers (CRCs), including malignant polyps, is associated with an 
increased risk of lymph node metastasis; however, methodologies for assessing tumor budding and grade were not uniform.32-36
REC-B
3 OF 10

header: PRINCIPLES OF PATHOLOGIC AND MOLECULAR REVIEW
content:
References
PLEASE NOTE that use of this Oncology Guidelines Content is governed by the End-User License Agreement, and you MAY NOT distribute this Content or use it with any artificial intelligence model or tool.

Rectal Cancer
Version 2.2026, 04/07/26 Â© 2026 Oncology GuidelinesÂź (Oncology GuidelinesÂź), All rights reserved. Oncology GuidelinesÂź and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
Lymph Node Evaluation
âą The AJCC and CAP recommend examination of a minimum of 12 lymph nodes to accurately stage rectal cancer.12,13,37 Sampling of 12 lymph nodes 
may not be achievable in patients who received preoperative chemotherapy. The literature lacks consensus as to the minimum number of lymph nodes 
needed to accurately identify stage II cancer. The minimum number of nodes has been reported as >7, >9, >13, >20, and >30.37-45 Most of these 
studies have combined rectal and colon cancers and reflect those cases with surgery as the initial treatment. Two studies confined only to rectal cancer 
have reported 14 and >10 lymph nodes as the minimum number to accurately identify stage II rectal cancer.41,44  The number of lymph nodes retrieved 
can vary with patient age, gender, tumor grade, and tumor site.38 For stage II (pN0) colon cancer, if <12 lymph nodes are initially identified, it is 
recommended that the pathologist go back to the specimen and resubmit more tissue of potential lymph nodes. If 12 lymph nodes are still not identified, 
a comment in the report should indicate that an extensive search for lymph nodes was undertaken. The mean number of lymph nodes retrieved from 
rectal cancers treated with neoadjuvant therapy is significantly less than those treated by surgery alone (13 vs. 19, P < .05; 7 vs. 10, P < .001).46,47 
If 12 lymph nodes is considered the number needed to accurately stage stage II tumors, then only 20% of patients treated with neoadjuvant therapy 
had adequate lymph node sampling.47 To date, the number of lymph nodes needed to accurately stage disease treated with neoadjuvant therapy is 
unknown. However, the clinical significance of this in the neoadjuvant setting is unknown, as postoperative therapy is indicated in all patients who 
receive preoperative therapy regardless of the surgical pathology results.
âą Isolated tumor clusters (ITC) are defined as single cells or small groups of cells measuring <0.2 mm. The biologic significance of ITC is currently not 
well characterized.48 Therefore, lymph nodes with ITC are defined as negative (N0) in the 9th edition of the AJCC staging system. However, the number 
of lymph nodes involved by ITC should be documented in the pathology report.
âą Micrometastatic tumor deposits measure between 0.2 mmâ2.0 mm and lymph nodes involved with micrometastatic deposits are considered positive 
lymph nodes.48
Evaluation of Mesorectum (total mesorectal excision, TME)
âą The pathologist should evaluate the quality (completeness) of the mesorectum (only for low rectal cancer - distal 2/3)49-51
REC-B
4 OF 10

header: PRINCIPLES OF PATHOLOGIC AND MOLECULAR REVIEW
content:
References

Version 2.2026, 04/07/26 Â© 2026 Oncology GuidelinesÂź (Oncology GuidelinesÂź), All rights reserved. Oncology GuidelinesÂź and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
Methods of Testing
âą Testing can be performed on formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded tissue or cytology material (preferred) or blood-based circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA)/
RNA. 
âą Repeat molecular testing should not be performed after standard cytotoxic chemotherapy, as significant molecular changes are rarely observed. 
Changes in the molecular profile can more commonly be seen after targeted therapies, and repeat testing may be considered to guide future targeted 
therapy decisions.
KRAS, NRAS, and BRAF Testing
âą All patients with metastatic CRC should have tumor genotyped for RAS (KRAS and NRAS) and BRAF mutations as part of MGPT at initial diagnosis 
of metastatic disease. Patients with any known KRAS mutation (exons 2, 3, and 4) or NRAS mutation (exons 2, 3, and 4) should not be treated with 
either cetuximab or panitumumab, unless given as part of a regimen targeting a KRAS G12C mutation.52-54 BRAF V600E mutation makes response to 
panitumumab or cetuximab highly unlikely.55-57
âą BRAF V600E mutation testing via immunohistochemistry (IHC) is also an option.
âą Testing for KRAS, NRAS, and BRAF mutations should be performed only in laboratories that are certified under the Clinical Laboratory Improvement 
Amendments of 1988 (CLIA-88) as qualified to perform high-complexity clinical laboratory (molecular pathology) testing. No specific methodology is 
recommended.
âą When using a multigene panel it is recommended to test the metastasis for genomic additional alterations. However, testing can be performed on the 
primary CRCs, as literature has shown that driver KRAS, NRAS, and BRAF mutations are similar in both specimen types.58
Microsatellite Instability (MSI) or Mismatch Repair Testing (MMR)
âą Universal MMRa or MSIa testing is recommended in all newly diagnosed patients with rectal cancer. See Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-
Risk Assessment: Colorectal, Endometrial, and Gastric.
âą The presence of a BRAF V600E mutation in the setting of MLH1 absence would preclude the diagnosis of Lynch syndrome (LS) in the vast majority 
of patients. However, approximately 1% of cancers with BRAF V600E mutations (and loss of MLH1) are LS. Caution should be exercised in excluding 
patients with a strong family history from germline screening in the case of BRAF V600E mutation.59
âą MMR or MSI testing should be performed only in CLIA-approved laboratories.
âą Testing for MSI involves looking for a change in the number of microsatellite repeats in tumor DNA compared to non-neoplastic tissue as evidence of 
deficient MMR mechanisms. This may be accomplished by multiplex polymerase chain reaction (PCR) followed by capillary electrophoresis or as part of 
a validated MGPT, the latter especially in patients with metastatic disease who require genotyping of KRAS, NRAS, and BRAF.
REC-B
5 OF 10
a	IHC for MMR and DNA analysis for MSI are different assays and measure different biological effects caused by dMMR function.

content:

Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
REC-B
6 OF 10
MSI or MMR Testing (continued)
âą IHC refers to interrogating tumor tissue for protein expression. IHC performed for MMR looks at the protein expression of four MMR genes (MLH1, 
MSH2, MSH6, and PMS2). Patients with pathogenic germline variants in these genes have LS. However, alterations in these genes also arise in 
sporadically acquired CRC. Tumors that show nuclear expression of all four MMR proteins are considered pMMR. Tumors that reveal loss of expression 
of any MMR protein are considered dMMR. To ensure clarity, interpretation of IHC result should be reported as either âretained for all MMR proteins,â or 
âloss of expression of (one or more of the MMR proteins)." The words âpositiveâ or ânegativeâ should be avoided, although there are a few caveats such 
as isolated loss of MSH6 following neoadjuvant treatment. MMR proteins function as heterodimers: MLH1/PMS2 and MSH2/MSH6. Therefore, loss of 
expression of one protein will usually result in the loss of the heterodimer partner.   
âą The pattern of lost protein expression guides further testing such as MLH1 promoter hypermethylation, or BRAF V600E or germline testing to assess for 
LS.
HER2 (ERBB2) Overexpression/Amplification Testing
âą Diagnostic testing is via IHC, fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH), or next-generation sequencing (NGS).
âą Positive by IHC is defined as: 3+ staining in >50% of tumor cells. 3+ staining is defined as an intense membrane staining that can be circumferential, 
basolateral, or lateral. Those who have a HER2 score of 2+ should be reflexed to FISH testing.60-62 HER2 (ERBB2) amplification detected by FISH is 
considered positive when the HER2 (ERBB2):CEP17 ratio is â„2 in >50% of the cells.60-62 NGS is another methodology for testing for HER2 (ERBB2) 
amplification.63
âą Anti-HER2 therapy with signal transduction inhibition (eg, trastuzumab/pertuzumab, trastuzumab/tucatinib) is indicated for patients with HER2 (ERBB2) 
overexpression/amplification, IHC 3+, IHC2+/ISH+, or HER2 (ERBB2) overexpressed/amplified (detected by NGS) cancers that are also KRAS, NRAS, 
and BRAF.
âą Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki is only indicated in HER2-overexpressed tumors (IHC 3+).
NTRK 1/2/3 Gene Fusions 
âą NTRK 1/2/3 gene fusions are extremely rare in CRCs.64 The overall incidence is approximately 0.35% in a cohort of 2314 CRCs, with NTRK 1/2/3 gene 
fusions confined to those tumors that are panâWT KRAS, NRAS, and BRAF. In one study of eight CRCs harboring NTRK 1/2/3 gene fusions, seven 
were found in the small subset that were dMMR (MLH1)/MSI-H.65 NTRK 1/2/3 gene fusions are more frequently found among patients with dMMR.
âą TRK inhibitors have been shown to have activity ONLY in those cases with NTRK 1/2/3 gene fusions, and NOT with NTRK 1/2/3 gene point mutations. 
âą Methodologies for detecting NTRK 1/2/3 gene fusions are IHC,66 FISH, DNA-based NGS, and RNA-based NGS.65,67 In one study, DNA-based 
sequencing showed an overall sensitivity and specificity of 81.1% and 99.9%, respectively, for detection of NTRK 1/2/3 gene fusions when compared 
to RNA-based sequencing and IHC showed an overall sensitivity of 87.9% and specificity of 81.1%. Since approximately one in five tumors identified 
as having an NTRK 1/2/3 gene fusion by IHC will be a false positive, tumors that test positive by IHC should be confirmed by RNA-based NGS. That 
same study commented that RNA-based sequencing appears to be the optimal way to approach NTRK 1/2/3 gene fusions, because the splicing out of 
introns simplifies the technical requirements of adequate coverage and because detection of RNA-level fusions provides direct evidence of functional 
transcription.67 However, selection of the appropriate assay for NTRK 1/2/3 gene fusion detection depends on tumor type and genes.

header: PRINCIPLES OF PATHOLOGIC AND MOLECULAR REVIEW
content:
POLE/POLD1
âą Polymerase genes, POLE and POLD1, encode proteins tasked with proofreading functions to recognize and correct mispaired bases incorporated 
during DNA replication. Pathogenic variants (PVs) within the exonuclease domains (ED) of POLE and POLD1 result in loss of this proofreading function 
leading to subsequent acquisition of numerous single nucleotide variants (SNVs).68,69 
âą Germline PVs within the ED of POLE and POLD1 predispose patients to multiple colorectal adenomas and carcinomas, resulting in polymerase 
proofreading-associated polyposis (PPAP)68,69 (see Oncology Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Colorectal, Endometrial, and 
Gastric). 
âą Somatic POLE mutations occur in approximately 2%â8% of patients with predominately MSS/pMMR CRC while somatic POLD1 mutations are 
extremely rare.68,70
âą NGS of CRCs in patients with either germline or somatic ED PVs demonstrate an ultra-hypermutated phenotype identified as extremely high tumor 
mutational burden (TMB >50 mut/Mb). TMB is calculated as the total number of somatic mutations per coding area of the tumor genome. Although 
calculations vary according to assay performed, TMB >10 mut/Mb is generally regarded as TMB-high (TMB-H).70,71
âą POLE/POLD1 mutations can be identified through single gene assays (PCR or Sanger sequencing). However, TMB calculation requires MGPT, which 
often includes concurrent POLE/POLD1 sequencing. As such, performing a MGPT on CRC tumor tissue has the advantage of not only identifying 
POLE/POLD1 mutations but also provides direct evidence of loss of proofreading function (TMB-H).70-72
âą Patients with CRC harboring POLE/POLD1 mutations have a more favorable prognosis, likely secondary to immune responses stimulated by the 
presence of numerous neoantigens produced as a consequence of aberrant proofreading function. Similarly, for these patients disease responds well to 
immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy.72-77
RET Gene Fusions
âą RET is a receptor tyrosine kinase that plays a critical role in the development and maintenance of neural and genitourinary tissues, primarily through 
downstream MAPK and PI3K signaling pathways.78
âą Germline activating PVs in RET lead to multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2 (MEN2) (see Oncology Guidelines for Neuroendocrine and Adrenal Tumors) 
and loss of function PVs  are associated with Hirschsprung disease and congenital abnormalities of the kidney and urinary tract.78
âą Somatic activating alterations in RET include point mutations as well as gene fusions and have been identified in a variety of tumors.78,79
âą In patients with CRC, activating RET gene fusions involving the C-terminal kinase domain lead to constitutive upregulation of RET kinase activity and 
subsequent promotion of cell proliferation and survival. The most common gene fusion partners reported include KIF5B, CCDC6, and NCOA4.78-81
âą The RET-targeted inhibitor, selpercatinib, is FDA-approved for patients with solid tumors harboring activating RET gene fusions.82
âą The presence of RET gene fusions can be interrogated through a variety of techniques, including IHC, FISH, polymerase chain reaction (PCR), and 
either DNA- or RNA-based NGS assays. RNA-based NGS assays are gene fusion agnostic and as such have the advantage of identifying RET gene 
fusions involving any partner gene.79-81
âą Caution should be used in interpreting RET gene fusion results from ctDNA/RNA assays following targeted therapy with EGFR inhibitors.
References

Version 2.2026, 04/07/26 Â© 2026 Oncology GuidelinesÂź (Oncology GuidelinesÂź), All rights reserved. Oncology GuidelinesÂź and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
REC-C
1 OF 9
Workup
âą Independent evaluation by the treating surgeon with either proctosigmoidoscopy or 
flexible sigmoidoscopy is recommended for all rectal tumors. Critical characteristics to be 
documented, in conjunction with DRE, include tumor size, distances from the anal verge 
and the anorectal ring, orientation within the rectal lumen (eg, anterior-posterior, laterality) 
and/or degree of circumferential involvement, extent of obstruction, extent of fixation to 
the rectal wall, degree of sphincter involvement, and sphincter tone.
Transanal Local Excision1
âą Criteria
<30% circumference of bowel; <3 cm in size; clear surgical margin (>3 mm); mobile, 
nonfixed; within 8 cm of anal verge; T1 only; endoscopically removed polyp with cancer 
or indeterminate pathology; no lymphovascular invasion or PNI; well to moderately 
differentiated; no evidence of lymphadenopathy on pretreatment imaging
Surgical technique includes full-thickness excision of rectal wall with goal of â„10 mm 
excision margin with an intact non-fragmented specimen.
âą When the lesion can be adequately localized to the rectum, local excision of more 
proximal lesions may be technically feasible using advanced techniques, such as 
transanal endoscopic microsurgery (TEM) or transanal minimally invasive surgery 
(TAMIS).
âą Local excision could be a viable option for patients who have a strong but incomplete 
response and are unable or unwilling to undergo standard TME surgery. The most 
suitable candidates would be those meeting the near complete response (nCR) criteria 
as outlined in the Principles of Nonoperative Management section (endoscopy, DRE, and 
MRI). 
âą The efficacy of local excision for patients who have achieved a clinical complete response 
(cCR) is not yet fully established. According to current data, cCR, as determined through 
MRI, DRE, and endoscopy, typically correlates with a durable response. Therefore, 
implementing local excision might introduce unnecessary risks without clear benefits. 
Given this uncertainty and potential for added risk, local excision is not routinely 
recommended for patients who have achieved a clear cCR.
Transabdominal Resection: Abdominoperineal resection or low anterior resection or 
coloanal anastomosis using TME
âą Management principles
The treating surgeon should be experienced in rectal cancer surgery, and specifically 
with TME. For patients with predicted positive margins based on preoperative imaging, 
or lateral pelvic lymph node involvement, the surgeon should be experienced in 
extended resections beyond the TME plane and have a multidisciplinary team available 
if necessary.2
The treating surgeon should assess the distal margin before initiating treatment by DRE 
Â± rigid or flexible endoscopy, particularly for non-palpable lesions.

header: PRINCIPLES OF SURGERY AND LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES
content:
Anticipated circumferential margins should be assessed by MRI (see Principles of 
Imaging, REC-A) prior to any required neoadjuvant therapy, and again considered 
prior to surgery. If margins are involved, assessment for feasibility of resection 
beyond the TME plane is required. Such an extended resection (Â± reconstruction) 
should involve careful preoperative planning and may require a multidisciplinary 
team.
For adequately staged, low-risk, upper-rectal T3, N0 tumors, surgery alone is an 
appropriate treatment option.
Remove primary tumor with adequate circumferential and distal margins.
Treat draining lymphatics by TME.
Sphincter preservation and restoration of organ integrity should be achieved 
without compromise of oncologic resection and consideration of anticipated patient 
functional outcome and quality of life.
âą TME is a standard component of radical rectal cancer surgery. TME reduces the 
positive radial margin and local recurrence rates.
Extend 4 to 5 cm below distal edge of tumors for an adequate mesorectal excision. 
In distal rectal cancers (ie, <5 cm from anal verge), negative distal bowel wall margin 
of 1 to 2 cm may be acceptable.
Full rectal mobilization allows for a negative distal margin and adequate mesorectal 
excision. 
Minimally invasive approaches (eg, laparoscopic, robotic) for resection of rectal 
cancer have been shown to be safe.3,4
There are no significant differences in disease-free survival and recurrence rates 
with minimally invasive approaches when compared to open resection.5-7
 âThe surgeon should have experience performing minimally invasive proctectomy 
with TME.
 âIt is not indicated for locally advanced disease with a threatened or high-risk 
circumferential margin based on staging. For these high-risk tumors, open surgery 
is preferred.
 âIt is not generally indicated for acute bowel obstruction or perforation from cancer.
 âThorough abdominal exploration is required.
âą Lymph node dissection8,9
Clinically suspicious nodes beyond the field of resection should be biopsied and/or 
removed, if possible. Extensive resection of M1 lymph nodes is not indicated.
Extended lymph node resection is not indicated in the absence of clinically 
suspected nodes.
References

REC-C
2 OF 9

header: PRINCIPLES OF SURGERY AND LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES
content:
Locoregional Therapies
âą Image-Guided Tumor Ablation10
Thermal ablation creates tumor cell death through deposition of tumoricidal heat (radiofrequency or microwave) or cold (cryoablation) in the tumor and 
surrounding margins.
Non-thermal ablation such as irreversible electroporation creates tumor cell death through electrical pulses that create irreversible membrane pores 
and cellular lysis/destruction.
âą Liver Tumor Ablation10-12
Thermal ablation can be considered alone, or in conjunction with surgery, in appropriately selected patients with small metastases that can be treated 
with margins. All original sites of disease need to be amenable to thermal ablation or resection.
Image-guided thermal ablation may be considered in selected surgical candidates or medically non-surgical candidates with small tumors that can be 
completely ablated with margins.
Image-guided thermal ablation can be considered in selected patients with recurrence after hepatectomy or ablation as long as all visible disease can 
be ablated with margins.10-12
Image-guided non-thermal ablation (irreversible electroporation) can be considered in patients whose tumors cannot be safely resected or ablated 
with margins due to proximity to central bile ducts or other structures that cannot be protected.
âą Lung Tumor Ablation13-15
Ablative techniques may be considered alone or in conjunction with resection for resectable disease. All original sites of disease need to be amenable 
to thermal ablation or resection.
Image-guided thermal ablation can also be considered when unresectable and amenable to complete thermal ablation.
Image-guided thermal ablation may be considered in selected surgical candidates with small tumors that can be completely ablated with margins.
Image-guided thermal ablation may be considered for recurrences after surgery or prior ablation as long as all visible disease is amenable to thermal 
ablation.
References

REC-C
3 OF 9

header: PRINCIPLES OF SURGERY AND LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES
content:
âą Arterially Directed Embolic Therapy
Hepatic transarterial radioembolization (TARE) with yttrium-90 (Y-90) microspheres16,17
 âY-90 radioembolization (radiation lobectomy approach) can be considered instead of portal vein embolization when hepatic metastatic disease is not 
optimally resectable based on insufficient remnant liver volume or when there is borderline resectable disease that would benefit from tumor downsizing 
and remnant hypertrophy.
 âHepatic TARE with Y-90 microspheres can be considered in selected patients with chemotherapy-resistant/refractory disease and with predominant hepatic 
metastases.
 âRadiation segmentectomy approach can be used for limited volume liver disease that is not amenable to resection or thermal ablation.
Transarterial chemoembolization (TACE)18
 âTACE involves hepatic artery catheterization to locally deliver chemotherapy in combination with arterial embolization.
 âTACE for hepatic metastatic tumors can be considered in highly selected cases with chemotherapy-resistant/refractory disease, preserved liver function, 
and predominant hepatic metastases.
 âThe most commonly accepted variation for the treatment of metastatic CRC involves the use of drug-eluting bead TACE (DEB-TACE) using irinotecan as 
the chemotherapeutic agent (DEBIRI). 
 âDEBIRI can be used along with irinotecan-based chemotherapy for unresectable liver-dominant disease.
âą External Beam Radiation Therapy (EBRT)
EBRT to the metastatic site can be considered in appropriately selected cases in which the patient has a limited number of metastases, including the liver or 
lung or other select locations; or the patient is symptomatic; or in the setting of a clinical trial.
The possible techniques include three-dimensional CRT (3D-CRT), intensity-modulated RT (IMRT), and stereotactive body RT (SBRT).
 âSBRT is an advanced technique of hypofractionated RT with photons that delivers large ablative doses of radiation. SBRT in the management of liver or 
lung metastases can be an alternative to ablation/embolization techniques or when these therapies have not been successful or are contraindicated.19-24
 âSBRT or other hypofractionated regimens with biologically effective dose (BED) 10 >100 Gy are preferred in the context of oligometastatic disease to 
provide durable local control. Final dosing should also take into account adjacent normal organs (see REC-E).
âą Hepatic Arterial Infusion (HAI)
Eligibility: 
 âMultidisciplinary experience with HAI therapy
 âCandidate for major surgery 
 âUnresectable colorectal liver metastases or resectable colorectal liver metastases at high risk for recurrence
 âTreated with at least one line of systemic chemotherapy 
 âNo extrahepatic disease; primary tumor may be in place 
 âSuitable hepatic arterial anatomy 
 âNo portal hypertension 
 âNo active viral hepatitis 
 âDirect bilirubin â€1.5 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase <2 times the upper limit of normal (ULN)
 âHAI chemotherapy cannot be delivered with concurrent bevacizumab or other vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) inhibitors
 âNo prior radiation to the liver
References

Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
REC-C
4 OF 9
Liver
âą Hepatic resection is the treatment of choice for resectable liver 
metastases from CRC.25
âą Complete resection must be feasible based on anatomic grounds and the 
extent of disease; maintenance of adequate hepatic  
function is required.26,27
âą There should be no unresectable extrahepatic sites of disease.28-30 
âą Partial debulking (R1/R2 resection) is not recommended.
âą Patients with resectable metastatic disease and primary tumor in place 
should have both sites resected with curative intent. 
These can be resected in one operation or as a staged approach, 
depending on the complexity of the hepatectomy or colectomy, 
comorbid diseases, surgical exposure, and surgeon expertise.
Staged procedures can be performed as liver-first or primary-first 
approaches.31
âą In the setting of neoadjuvant therapy, placement of pre-treatment fiducial 
marker(s) in smaller lesions may be considered.
âą Re-resection and re-ablation can be considered in selected patients.32
âą When hepatic metastatic disease is not optimally resectable based on 
insufficient remnant liver volume, approaches using preoperative portal 
vein embolization or staged liver resections can be considered. 
âą At the time of surgery, ablative techniques may be considered alone or 
in conjunction with resection.25 All original sites of disease should be 
amenable to thermal ablation or resection.
âą Thermal ablation may be considered in selected surgical candidates with 
small tumors that can be completely ablated with margins.
âą Arterially directed catheter therapy, and in particular Y-90 microsphere 
radioembolization, is an option in selected patients with chemotherapy-
resistant/-refractory disease and with predominant hepatic metastases.
âą Ablative EBRT may be considered in selected cases or in the setting 
of a clinical trial and should not be used indiscriminately in patients 
with potentially surgically resectable disease or disease that can be 
percutaneously ablated with margins.
âą For liver metastases, in general, resection may be prioritized over local 
thermal ablation. However, for select patients with small lesions (â€3 cm), 
thermal ablation should be prioritized, when feasible, due to equivalent 
outcomes and reduced toxicity compared to surgery.33 SBRT can be 
considered in select cases where other local therapy options are not 
possible or in patients who prefer a noninvasive approach.
Lung
âą Complete resection based on the anatomic location and extent of disease 
with maintenance of adequate function is required.34-37
âą The primary tumor must have been resected for cure (R0).
âą Resectable extrapulmonary metastases do not preclude resection.38-41
âą Re-resection and re-ablation can be considered in selected patients.42
âą Ablative techniques may be considered alone or in conjunction with 
resection for resectable disease. All original sites of disease need to be 
amenable to thermal ablation or resection. 
âą Ablative techniques can also be considered when unresectable and 
amenable to complete thermal ablation. 
âą Thermal ablation may be considered in selected surgical candidates with 
small tumors that can be completely ablated with margins.
âą Patients with resectable synchronous metastases can be resected 
synchronously or using a staged approach.
âą Ablative EBRT may be considered in selected cases or in the setting 
of a clinical trial and should not be used indiscriminately in patients 
with potentially surgically resectable disease or disease that can be 
percutaneously ablated.
âą For lung metastases, surgery, thermal ablation, and SBRT can be 
considered based on multidisciplinary discussion and patient preference.
PRINCIPLES OF SURGERY AND LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES
CRITERIA FOR RESECTABILITY OF METASTASES AND LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES WITHIN SURGERY
References
Local therapies for liver and lung oligometastases should be selected through multidisciplinary review based on technical feasibility, patient 
comorbidities, and disease distribution.

REC-C
5 OF 9
Evaluation for Conversion to Resectable or Ablatable Disease
âą Re-evaluation for resection and/or ablation should be considered in otherwise unresectable patients after 2 months of preoperative chemotherapy and 
every 2 months thereafter.43-46
âą Metastatic tumor(s) with a higher likelihood of being converted to resectable and/or ablatable are those in which the initial disease is confined to limited 
sites.
âą When considering whether disease has been converted to resectable and/or ablatable, all original sites need to be amenable to treatment.47   
Preoperative chemotherapy regimens with high response rates should be considered for patients with potentially convertible disease.48
PRINCIPLES OF SURGERY AND LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES
CRITERIA FOR RESECTABILITY OF METASTASES AND LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES WITHIN SURGERY

Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
REC-C
6 OF 9
PRINCIPLES OF SURGERY AND LOCOREGIONAL THERAPIES
ENDOSCOPIC SUBMUCOSAL DISSECTION (ESD) 
Therapeutic Principles
âą ESD is a minimally invasive, organ-preserving procedure that can provide 
curative resection for early rectal cancers by removing these lesions en 
bloc. 
âą ESD usually involves using an injection with submucosal dissection of the 
lesion to achieve complete en bloc resection. 
âą ESD can provide either curative resection after en bloc removal or, if not 
curative, can provide an accurate pathologic staging of disease.
Pre-ESD Endoscopic Evaluation
âą Successful curative resection of rectal lesions requires a thorough 
assessment of the lesion, including subclassification of laterally spreading 
tumor (LST), as well as vessel, surface, and pit pattern.49
âą The morphology of all lesions should be described using the Paris 
classification, which has been shown to correlate to the likelihood of 
invasive cancer.50
âą ESD should be performed at a high-volume center by an experienced 
endoscopist or surgeon.49
âą EUS or MRI should be used in the rectum prior to resection when 
suspicious features of deep submucosal invasion are present.49
Criteria for Resection
âą ESD with en bloc resection should be considered for rectal lesions at risk 
for submucosally invasive cancer51,52
Type V Kudo pit pattern 
Depressed component (Paris 0âIIc)
Complex morphology (0âIs or 0âIIa+Is)
Nongranular, LST â„20 mm in size
Granular, laterally spreading tumor â„30 mm in size
âą Residual or recurrent colorectal adenomas
âą Rectal lesions that have surface features (vascular or pit pattern) 
suggestive of advanced dysplasia or early submucosally invasive 
carcinoma
Curative Resection
âą Based on both the Japanese53 and European 49 guidelines, resection of 
rectal lesions can be considered curative when:
Negative circumferential and deep vertical tumor margins 
Submucosal invasion depth <1000 Â”m
Absence of lymphovascular invasion
Absent or grade 1 (low-grade) tumor budding
Well (G1) to moderately (G2) differentiated tumor histology 
 âIf a removed lesion does not meet the above criteria, a 
multidisciplinary team including an endoscopist, surgeon, and 
pathologist should use a shared decision-making process to decide 
whether to proceed with surgery versus intensive surveillance.
Surveillance After ESD51
âą Risk of lymph node metastasis after curative resection of superficial T1 
rectal cancer with submucosal invasion <1000 Â”m is estimated to be 
3%â6%.
âą Flexible sigmoidoscopy is recommended 3â6 months after ESD assuming 
prior colonoscopy.
âą Second follow-up endoscopy is recommended 3â6 months after first 
surveillance exam or colonoscopy if 1 year from ESD.
âą Surveillance using flexible sigmoidoscopy is recommended every 6 
months for a total of 5 years from ESD.
âą EUS or pelvis MRI with contrast is recommended every 3â6 months for 2 
years, then every 6 months to complete 5 years.
Surgery vs. ESD
âą Prior retrospective studies have found that ESD and TEM/TAMIS do not 
differ in terms of outcomes, including local recurrence, R0 resection rate, 
and adverse events.54-56
âą The decision between surgery and ESD should be determined based on 
the patientâs candidacy for surgery, availability of expertise in ESD, and 
patient preference.
References

Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.
Oncology Guidelines Index
Table of Contents
Discussion
Not every patient with rectal cancer requires trimodality treatmentâtrials with adaptive designs have demonstrated some patients will have favorable 
outcomes with selective usage of radiation or selective usage of surgery, based on reassessment of response during therapy.1,2 
The regimens used in patients who will undergo or have undergone surgery include both concurrent chemotherapy/RT and chemotherapy alone. 
Perioperative treatment is recommended for up to a total of 3 to 6 months.
Perioperative Chemotherapy:
âą Modified FOLFOX63,4,5 
Oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV, Day 1,a Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV Day 1,b Fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus on Day 1, followed by 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 days 
(total 2400 mg/m2 over 46â48 hours) continuous infusion. Repeat every 2 weeks to a total of 6 months perioperative therapy.
âą CAPEOX6,7 
Oxaliplatin 130 mg/m2 IV Day 1.a Capecitabine 1000 mg/m2 PO twice daily for 14 days every 3 weeks. Repeat every 3 weeks to a total of  
6 months perioperative therapy.
âą FOLFIRINOX8,c  
Oxaliplatin 85 mg/mÂČ IV on Day 1,a Leucovorin 400 mg/mÂČ IV over 2 hours on Day 1,b Irinotecan 180 mg/mÂČ IV over 30â90 minutes on Day 1, 
Fluorouracil 400 mg/mÂČ IV push Day 1, Fluorouracil 1200 mg/mÂČ/day x 2 days (total 2400 mg/mÂČ over 46 hours) continuous infusion.  
Repeat every 2 weeks.
âą Modified FOLFIRINOX9,c 
Oxaliplatin 85 mg/mÂČ IV on Day 1,a Leucovorin 400 mg/mÂČ IV over 2 hours on Day 1,b Irinotecan 150 mg/mÂČ IV over 30â90 minutes on Day 1, 
Fluorouracil 1200 mg/mÂČ/day x 2 days (total 2400 mg/mÂČ over 46 hours) continuous infusion. Repeat every 2 weeks.
Dosing Schedules for Concurrent Chemotherapy/RT:
âą Fluorouracil (continuous infusion)10 + RT 
Fluorouracil 225 mg/m2 IV over 24 hours daily on Days 1â5 or Days 1â7 for 5 weeks with RT
âą Capecitabine11,12 + RT 
Capecitabine 825 mg/m2 PO BID, MondayâFriday, on days of radiation treatment only, throughout the duration of RT (typically 28â30 treatment days)
REC-D
1 OF 2

header: PRINCIPLES OF PERIOPERATIVE THERAPY
content:
References
a	Oxaliplatin may be given either over 2 hours, or may be infused over a shorter time at a rate of 1 mg/m2/min. Leucovorin infusion should match infusion time of 
oxaliplatin. Cercek A, Park V, Yaeger R, et al. Faster FOLFOX: oxaliplatin can be safely infused at a rate of 1 mg/m2/min. J Oncol Pract 2016;12:e548-553.
b	Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 is the equivalent of levoleucovorin 200 mg/m2.
c FOLFIRINOX is recommended instead of FOLFOXIRI because FOLFOXIRI uses a high dose of fluorouracil (3200 mg/mÂČ over 48 hours). Patients in the United 
States have been shown to have greater toxicity with fluorouracil. The dose of fluorouracil (2400 mg/mÂČ over 46 hours) is a starting dose consistent with the dose 
recommended in FOLFOX or FOLFIRI and should be strongly considered for U.S. patients.
